past_medical_history: Iron deficiency anemia, diagnosed 2 years ago, resolved with supplementation
Vitamin D deficiency, diagnosed 1 year ago, on supplementation
Anxiety disorder, diagnosed 3 years ago, managed with sertraline
Episode of right hand numbness and tingling 14 months ago lasting approximately 3 weeks, attributed to carpal tunnel syndrome at urgent care, no imaging performed
family_history: Mother with Hashimoto thyroiditis, diagnosed at age 40
No family history of multiple sclerosis or other neurological conditions

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in a second-floor apartment with elevator access
social_history.exercise: Previously ran 3 times per week, stopped 2 weeks ago due to leg weakness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect mildly anxious. Speech fluent without dysarthria. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Attention preserved — serial sevens performed without error. Short-term memory intact (3/3 objects at 5 minutes). No cognitive complaints aside from subjective difficulty concentrating attributed to fatigue.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Visual acuity 20/20 left eye, 20/25 right eye. Color desaturation noted in the right eye compared to left on red desaturation testing. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. No relative afferent pupillary defect detected. Extraocular movements full, no nystagmus at primary gaze but 3-4 beats of horizontal nystagmus on sustained leftward gaze. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 strength bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, and intrinsic hand muscles. Lower extremities: 4+/5 hip flexion bilaterally, 4/5 knee extension bilaterally, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion bilaterally. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact in upper extremities bilaterally. Reduced vibration sense at both great toes (dampened compared to ankles). Decreased light touch and pinprick sensation over the anterior thighs and medial calves bilaterally, more prominent on the left. Temperature sensation not formally tested. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Upper extremities: biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Lower extremities: patellar 3+ bilaterally with crossed adductor response on the left, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Right plantar response extensor (upgoing), left plantar response equivocal.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing mildly impaired on the left with slight lateral deviation on ascent. No dysdiadochokinesia. No intention tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic gait with reduced stride length. Circumducts slightly on the left. Tandem gait impaired — steps off line twice. Romberg positive with moderate sway, corrects without falling.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive — patient reports electric shock sensation radiating down spine with neck flexion. No meningismus. No Uhthoff phenomenon elicited during exam.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive weakness in both legs and difficulty with urination over the past two weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Tyrone Coleman is a 38-year-old right-handed woman who presents to the hospital with a two-week history of progressive bilateral leg weakness and new urinary symptoms. He reports that approximately two weeks ago he began noticing that his legs felt heavy and stiff, particularly after climbing the stairs to his apartment. Over the ensuing days, the weakness gradually worsened to the point that he needed to hold onto the railing with both hands and pause halfway up. About ten days ago he noticed an uncomfortable band-like tightness around his waist at the level of his navel, which he initially attributed to bloating. Around the same time, he developed urinary urgency with occasional episodes of near-incontinence, requiring his to rush to the bathroom multiple times per hour. He has not had any frank urinary incontinence but describes a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying. He stopped running entirely due to his legs feeling weak and uncoordinated. He denies any back pain, recent trauma, or heavy lifting. He recalls an episode approximately 14 months ago when he developed numbness and tingling in his right hand that lasted about three weeks. He was seen at an urgent care clinic and told it was likely carpal tunnel syndrome; no imaging or nerve conduction studies were performed, and the symptoms eventually resolved spontaneously. Over the past few days, he has also noticed that his vision in the right eye seems slightly less vivid than the left, particularly for colors, though he has not had any frank vision loss or pain with eye movements. He reports increased fatigue over the past month, requiring naps after work, which is unusual for her. He denies fever, weight loss, night sweats, joint pain, skin rash, or oral ulcers. He has no recent travel history or sick contacts. He has never had an MRI or lumbar puncture. His partner urged his to come to the emergency department after he nearly fell descending the stairs yesterday evening.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, predominantly along the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Largest lesion 12 mm in the right periventricular region
findings.borders: Relatively well-defined, ovoid morphology, some oriented perpendicular to ventricles

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, adjacent to the body of the lateral ventricle
Juxtacortical white matter, right frontal lobe
Dorsal cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, seen on sagittal sequences
findings.mass_effect: Mild cord swelling at this level

additional_observations: No infratentorial lesions identified on this study
Corpus callosum appears normal without definite callosal-septal interface lesions
Ventricles and sulci are normal for age
mri.impression: Several periventricular ovoid lesions are noted, some with orientation perpendicular to the ventricles though classic Dawson finger morphology is not definitively established. A single enhancing lesion in the left periventricular white matter suggests active inflammation. A juxtacortical lesion in the right frontal lobe is also present. A cervical cord lesion at C3-C4 is identified. The combination of periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord lesions involves at least 3 of 4 characteristic MS topographies (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). The presence of both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions may satisfy criteria for dissemination in time. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 152 mEq/L (H), 25-OH Vitamin D 29 ng/mL (L).

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: 2 bands unique to CSF, not present in serum

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 8 cells/uL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 50.0 mg/dL (reference: 15-45). Glucose: 62.3 mg/dL (reference: 40-70) (ratio 0.67 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). oligoclonal_bands: 2 bands unique to CSF, not present in serum. IgG_index: 0.68 (reference: 0.28-0.66). IgG_synthesis_rate: 9.2 mg/day (reference: <8.0 mg/day). myelin_basic_protein: 5.8 ng/mL (reference: <4.0). cytology: No malignant cells identified.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: P100 latency - Right eye 118 ms ms (H), Interocular latency difference 16 ms ms (H).

findings.location: Dorsal cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 (as seen on brain MRI sagittal sequences)
findings.borders: Partially defined, does not span full cross-section of cord

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T6-T7, dorsolateral
findings.size: Spanning less than one vertebral segment

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (no lesion spanning 3 or more vertebral segments)
No disc herniations or spinal canal stenosis
output.impression: Two short-segment spinal cord lesions identified: one at C3-C4 with faint enhancement suggesting recent activity, and one at T6-T7 without enhancement suggesting a prior subclinical lesion. The short-segment, peripheral, and dorsal distribution is typical for multiple sclerosis. The T6-T7 lesion likely correlates with the patient's sensory level and band-like tightness at the waist. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Average RNFL thickness 82 um um (H), Temporal RNFL 58 um um (H), Ganglion cell layer thickness 72 um um (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Ganglion cell layer thickness: 72 um um (H)

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral — previously seen lesions unchanged
findings.size: Largest lesion 12 mm right periventricular, stable

additional_observations: New infratentorial lesion in the left middle cerebellar peduncle not present on prior study
Cervical cord lesion at C3-C4 shows interval decrease in swelling and signal change
No new spinal cord lesions identified
output.impression: Follow-up MRI at 3 months demonstrates a new enhancing infratentorial lesion in the left middle cerebellar peduncle, representing new disease activity despite interval resolution of the previously enhancing periventricular lesion. This new activity despite no treatment underscores the need for prompt initiation of disease-modifying therapy. Stable periventricular and juxtacortical lesions. Cervical cord lesion shows interval improvement. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: McDonald 2017 criteria for multiple sclerosis diagnosis

results.title: McDonald 2017 Criteria for MS Diagnosis
results.source: Thompson AJ et al. Lancet Neurol. 2018;17(2):162-173
results.summary: Revised diagnostic criteria requiring demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS requires lesions in 2 or more of 4 characteristic CNS areas: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord. DIT can be demonstrated by simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or new T2/enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, or CSF oligoclonal bands.

output.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow MS diagnosis with: (1) 2 or more clinical attacks with objective evidence of 2 lesions; (2) 2 attacks with 1 objective lesion plus DIS criteria; (3) 1 attack with objective evidence of 2 lesions plus DIT criteria; or (4) 1 attack with 1 objective lesion plus both DIS and DIT criteria. Importantly, CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for DIT in appropriate clinical scenarios.
output.interpretation: Reference criteria for MS diagnosis. See special_tests results for application to this patient.

interactions: No significant interaction with vitamin D3
warnings: Monitor complete blood count every 6 months — risk of lymphopenia
GI side effects common initially — consider taking with food
Check JC virus antibody status before initiation
alternatives: Ocrelizumab — higher efficacy, IV infusion every 6 months
Natalizumab — high efficacy but JC virus risk
Glatiramer acetate — lower efficacy but favorable safety profile
output.interpretation: Drug interaction check for proposed disease-modifying therapy. No significant interactions with current medications identified.

differential.key_features: AQP4 antibodies negative, spinal cord lesions are short-segment not longitudinally extensive, no area postrema syndrome
MOG antibodies negative, presentation more consistent with typical MS pattern
Normal inflammatory markers, lesion distribution typical for demyelination, no systemic vasculitis features
No leptomeningeal enhancement, no pulmonary findings, normal ACE level expected
Vitamin B12 level normal at 445 pg/mL

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including prior neurological episodes suggestive of dissemination in time
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Episode of right hand numbness 14 months ago — possible prior clinical attack

optimal_actions.action: Perform complete neurological examination with attention to optic nerve function, upper motor neuron signs, and sensory level
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral leg weakness, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, Lhermitte sign, subtle right eye color desaturation, sensory level, and spastic gait

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain and cervical spine with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord lesions with one enhancing lesion — suggestive of demyelinating disease meeting partial McDonald criteria for DIS

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present (2 bands, borderline), borderline elevated IgG index, mild pleocytosis, elevated myelin basic protein

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, TSH, vitamin D to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal except mildly low vitamin D — no evidence of systemic autoimmune disease or metabolic cause

optimal_actions.action: Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both antibodies negative, supporting MS diagnosis over mimics

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to evaluate for subclinical optic nerve involvement
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in right eye confirming subclinical optic nerve demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI thoracic spine with contrast to evaluate for additional spinal cord lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Additional short-segment lesion at T6-T7, further supporting DIS

optimal_actions.action: Initiate acute treatment with IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3-5 days for acute relapse
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement in leg weakness and urinary symptoms expected over days to weeks

optimal_actions.action: Discuss and initiate disease-modifying therapy after acute treatment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selection of appropriate DMT based on disease activity, patient preferences, and risk profile

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neuro-ophthalmology for OCT and formal visual assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: RNFL thinning in right eye confirming prior optic nerve injury

optimal_actions.action: Arrange urology consultation or post-void residual assessment for urinary symptoms

critical_actions: Obtain MRI brain and spinal cord with gadolinium contrast
Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands
Order AQP4 and MOG antibodies to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Treat acute relapse with IV corticosteroids
Recognize prior episode of hand numbness as possible first clinical attack (dissemination in time)
contraindicated_actions: Starting chronic immunosuppression (e.g., cyclophosphamide, azathioprine) without first confirming MS diagnosis and excluding NMOSD
Initiating natalizumab without first checking JC virus antibody status
Performing a brain biopsy when clinical and paraclinical evidence is sufficient for diagnosis
Attributing symptoms to anxiety or functional disorder without completing appropriate diagnostic workup
Prolonged oral corticosteroid taper beyond standard acute relapse treatment
key_reasoning_points: This is a first recognized clinical presentation but history reveals a prior episode (right hand numbness 14 months ago) that was likely a subclinical MS attack, providing clinical dissemination in time
MRI shows lesions in 3 of 4 characteristic MS topographies (periventricular, juxtacortical, spinal cord) meeting dissemination in space criteria
The single enhancing lesion alongside non-enhancing lesions provides radiographic dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions per 2017 McDonald criteria)
CSF findings are supportive but borderline — only 2 oligoclonal bands and borderline IgG index — making this moderate difficulty rather than straightforward
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies are critical for excluding important MS mimics, particularly given the spinal cord involvement
The Lhermitte sign is a highly suggestive clinical finding for cervical spinal cord demyelination
Vitamin D deficiency is epidemiologically associated with MS and should be corrected, but is not diagnostic
The band-like sensation around the waist corresponds to the thoracic cord lesion (MS hug) and helps localize pathology

metadata.clinical_notes: Moderate difficulty MS case featuring a first recognized clinical event with retrospective evidence of prior subclinical attack. Borderline CSF findings add diagnostic uncertainty. Key challenge is recognizing dissemination in time through clinical history and MRI features.
